meta.title: ICH Q5 Series — Biologics Quality Requirements
sources: ICH Q5A(R2) — Viral Safety Evaluation of Biotechnology Products (2023)
ICH Q5B — Analysis of the Expression Construct in Cells Used for Production of r-DNA Derived Protein Products (1995)
ICH Q5C — Stability Testing of Biotechnological/Biological Products (1995)
ICH Q5D — Derivation and Characterisation of Cell Substrates (1997)
ICH Q5E — Comparability of Biotechnological/Biological Products Subject to Changes in Their Manufacturing Process (2004)
meta.scope: Proteins, polypeptides, mAbs, conjugates, viral vectors, VLPs, vaccines produced from cell lines of human or animal origin via rDNA or non-recombinant methods

viral_safety.description: Three-pronged approach: testing cell banks and bulk, evaluating viral clearance, selecting appropriate cell lines

rules.requirement: MCB must be tested for retroviruses and other endogenous viruses using retrovirus tests (TEM, RT assay, infectivity assays, cocultivation)

In vitro cell culture infectivity assays must be performed on MCB, WCB, and LIVCA cells using indicator cell lines from the species of origin, human diploid cells (MRC-5), and monkey kidney cells (Vero)
For MCB, WCB, and LIVCA qualification, 28-day testing on permissive cells must be performed with at least one sub-passage at two weeks
Assay duration may be reduced to 14 days with justification based on risk assessment considering cell substrate, cultivation period, use of animal-derived materials, and viral clearance level
In vivo testing may be performed based on risk assessment but is not necessary for extensively used well-characterised cell lines (CHO, NS0, SP2/0) based on prior knowledge
Non-targeted NGS is encouraged as replacement for in vivo assays due to breadth and sensitivity of virus detection and to replace, reduce, and refine animal testing
Tests for specific viruses must be defined based on viral contamination risk assessment considering origin of cells, biological raw materials, and exposure to human or animal derived materials
For cell lines with exposure to bovine serum and porcine trypsin, tests for human, bovine, and porcine viruses must be performed
For rodent cell lines, MAP, RAP, and/or HAP tests (or equivalent NAT/NGS) must be performed to detect species-specific viruses
TEM is generally recommended for characterisation of cell banks to detect retroviral particles and other agents
If cell line produces retroviral particles, both TEM and PCR-based RT assay (PERT) should be performed on cells and clarified supernatant
Positive RT result or positive TEM result must be followed by testing to detect infectious retroviruses in permissive cells including human cell lines with sensitive readout assays
NGS method validation/qualification must include method validation and matrix-specific verification, with performance criteria per ICH Q2 principles

NGS workflow must address: (1) sample pre-treatment and virus enrichment, (2) nucleic acid extraction efficiency, (3) sequencing platform selection, (4) bioinformatics analysis against comprehensive viral database
Manufacturers must develop programs to routinely test for adventitious viruses in production batches at the unprocessed bulk stage
Adventitious virus testing must be routinely applied to each unprocessed bulk harvest using in vitro assays with indicator cell lines or non-targeted NGS
Indicator cell cultures must be observed for 28 days including at least one sub-passage at two weeks; may be reduced to 14 days with justification
If adventitious viruses are detected at unprocessed bulk stage, the product harvest should not be used unless justified per Section 5 action plan
For continuous manufacturing processes, final release of a batch must include documented absence of viral contamination for the entire cultivation period
Viral clearance evaluation must include both process evaluation (using relevant/specific model viruses) and process characterisation (using non-specific model viruses)

Clearance studies must use viruses representing a wide range of physicochemical properties; process should be assessed for ability to clear at least three different viruses with differing characteristics
Viral clearance studies must be conducted in a separate laboratory equipped for virological work, not in production facility
Scale-down model must represent the production process as closely as possible and demonstrate comparable yield and purity
For chromatographic steps, scale-down model must match column bed-height, linear flow-rate, flow-rate-to-bed-volume ratio, buffer/resin types, pH, temperature, conductivity, and product concentration
Each production step must be individually assessed for ability to remove or inactivate virus; virus titre before and after each step must be determined
For inactivation steps, time-dependent inactivation curve must be constructed with samples at different times including at least one time point less than minimum exposure time and greater than zero
Reproducible viral clearance must be confirmed in at least two independent experiments unless justified by prior knowledge
An effective viral clearance step generally gives reproducible reduction of virus load in the order of 4 log10 or more shown by at least two independent studies
Reduction in virus load less than 1 log10 is considered negligible and should be ignored unless justified
Individual reduction factor used for overall calculation must be greater than 1 log10
Overall reduction factor is the sum of individual logarithmic reduction factors; must not add factors from steps with similar inactivation mechanisms
Downstream process should implement at least two distinct complementary viral clearance steps, one of which should provide effective clearance of non-enveloped virus
Virus reduction factor = log10 of (virus load pre-step / virus load post-step), accounting for both titres and volumes
95% confidence limits for reduction factors should be calculated for relevant and specific model virus clearance studies
Chromatography media/resin lifetime use should be indicated and parameters with potential impact on viral clearance defined; clearance studies should support media/resin reuse
Case A (no virus/VLP detected): Perform virus removal and inactivation studies with non-specific model viruses only
Case B (rodent retrovirus only): Process evaluation using specific model virus (e.g., MLV) plus non-specific clearance evaluation; provide RVLP quantification from 3+ lots of purified bulk
Case B — CHO cells: For non-infectious endogenous retroviruses, value of ≤10^-4 particles/dose is considered acceptable for RVLPs if testing fails to identify infectious retroviruses
Case C (virus not infectious to humans): Use identified virus for removal/inactivation studies; time-dependent inactivation data required; test purified bulk from 3+ batches at pilot/commercial scale
Case D (virus infectious to humans): Product acceptable only under exceptional circumstances; virus removal/inactivation studies with identified virus required; purified bulk from 3+ batches tested
Case E (unclassifiable virus detected): Product usually considered unacceptable; discuss with regulatory authorities before proceeding
Case F (production virus used): Clearance of production virus must be demonstrated; test for absence in each purified bulk unless justified by robust excess clearance
Platform validation approach may be used when manufacturer develops similar products on established and well-characterised processes (same platform technology)
Prior knowledge for platform validation must include understanding of mechanism, process parameters, virus-product interactions, composition of process intermediate, and limitations per Section 6.4
Detergent inactivation platform conditions: ≥0.5% Triton X-100 with clarified HCCF for ≥60 minutes at ≥15°C effectively inactivates XMLV
Low pH inactivation platform conditions: ≤pH 3.6, ≥15°C for ≥30 minutes at ≤500 mmol/L NaCl effectively inactivates XMLV
Virus filtration: At least one confirmatory product-specific parvovirus validation run under worst-case conditions required when using prior knowledge
HCCF must be clarified including filtration step of ≤0.2 μm nominal pore size before detergent inactivation
Whenever significant changes are made in upstream or downstream manufacturing processes, viral clearance must be re-evaluated
CM viral safety strategy must be based on risk assessment of potential contamination sources, process removal capability, and testing capability across extended culture durations
For CM, appropriate process monitoring must detect (a) process disturbance impacting viral clearance and (b) adventitious virus contamination

For genetically engineered viral vectors and viral vector-derived products, Annex 6 testing scheme (Table A-5) applies: test virus seeds, unprocessed bulk, and drug substance for adventitious, endogenous, production, and replication competent viruses
Replication Competent Virus (RCV) testing is recommended at multiple stages of manufacture: virus seed/bank, each unprocessed bulk harvest, and drug substance/product
Production virus should be quantified at unprocessed bulk stage using at least three cell culture lots/batches to determine target for viral clearance
rules.reference: Q5A(R2) Annex 6, Table A-5 footnote e

expression_construct.description: Analysis of expression construct for r-DNA derived protein products

rules.requirement: Manufacturer must describe origin of nucleotide sequence coding for the protein, identification and source of the cell from which the sequence was obtained, and methods used to prepare the DNA
Detailed component map and complete annotated sequence of the plasmid must be provided, indicating sequenced regions and those taken from literature
Assembly steps of expression construct must be described in detail including source and function of all component parts: origins of replication, antibiotic resistance genes, promoters, enhancers
Nucleotide sequence of the coding region and associated flanking regions including junctions of insertion must be determined by DNA sequencing
Restriction endonuclease mapping or other suitable techniques must analyse expression construct for copy number, insertions/deletions, and number of integration sites
For extrachromosomal expression systems, the percent of host cells retaining the expression construct must be determined
Protein coding sequence of the expression construct must be verified in MCB; for extrachromosomal systems, construct must be isolated and sequence verified without further cloning

Expression construct of production cells must be analysed once (at MCB level per III.B) except that protein coding sequence in production cells may be verified by either nucleic acid testing or analysis of final protein product
Limit for in vitro cell age for production must be based on data from production cells expanded at pilot plant or full scale to the proposed in vitro cell age or beyond
Increases in defined limit for in vitro cell age must be supported by data from cells expanded to the proposed new limit
Analytical methodologies for sequence confirmation must be validated with documentation including estimates of limits of detection for variant sequences
Where production cells have multiple integrated copies, examination of mRNA or cDNA transcription product may be more appropriate than analysis of genomic DNA

biotech_stability.description: Stability testing requirements specific to biotechnological/biological products (supplement to Q1A)

rules.requirement: Stability data must be provided on at least 3 batches representative of manufacturing scale; minimum 6 months data at time of submission for storage periods >6 months
Pilot-plant scale batch data may be provided at submission with commitment to place first 3 manufacturing scale batches into long-term stability program after approval
Stability information must be provided on at least 3 batches of final container product representative of manufacturing scale
Product expiration dating must be based on real-time/real-temperature data; continuing updates of initial stability data should occur during review process
Manufacturers must identify critical intermediates and generate in-house data and process limits assuring their stability within bounds of the developed process
Manufacturer must propose a stability-indicating profile that provides assurance that changes in identity, purity, and potency of the product will be detected
Methods comprising the stability-indicating profile must be validated and data available for review at time of submission

When product has definable and measurable biological activity, potency testing must be part of stability studies; results reported in units calibrated against national or international reference standards
For conjugated products, dissociation of active ingredient from carrier/adjuvant must be examined in real-time/real-temperature studies including shipment conditions
Purity of biotech product must be assessed by more than one method; tests should focus on degradation products (deamidation, oxidation, aggregation, fragmentation)
Analytical methods for purity characterisation should include SDS-PAGE, immunoelectrophoresis, Western blot, IEF, HPLC (gel filtration, RP, IEX, affinity), and peptide mapping
Limits of acceptable degradation must be proposed and justified based on levels observed in material used in preclinical and clinical studies
Visual appearance, particulates, pH, moisture level (for lyophilised products), and sterility testing must be monitored in stability program for drug product
Sterility testing or container/closure integrity testing must be performed at minimum initially and at end of proposed shelf-life
Since biotech products need precisely defined storage temperatures, real-time/real-temperature stability studies may be confined to the proposed storage temperature
Accelerated and stress conditions for biotech products should be carefully selected on a case-by-case basis; standard accelerated conditions from Q1A may not be appropriate
For liquid products (other than sealed ampoules), stability studies must include samples in inverted/horizontal position (in contact with closure) and upright position
For multi-dose vials, closure must demonstrate capability of withstanding repeated insertions and withdrawals while maintaining potency, purity, and quality
Stability of freeze-dried products after reconstitution must be demonstrated for conditions and maximum storage period specified on labelling
For shelf-lives ≤1 year: test monthly for first 3 months, then at 3-month intervals; for >1 year: every 3 months in year 1, every 6 months in year 2, then annually

Each product must retain its specifications within established limits for safety, purity, and potency throughout proposed shelf-life; specifications derived from all available data using appropriate statistical methods
Precisely defined storage temperatures must be recommended on labelling; include protection against light and/or humidity where appropriate
Matrixing and bracketing may be applied per Q1D principles but should not be applied to samples where differences may affect stability (different strengths, different container/closures)

cell_substrate.description: Derivation and characterisation of cell substrates used for production

rules.requirement: Complete documented history of the cell substrate must be maintained including parental cell line history, manipulation records, and all events during R&D phases
For human cell lines: tissue/organ of origin, ethnic and geographical origin, age, sex, health/medical history of donor, and results of tests for pathogenic agents must be described
For animal cell lines: species, strains, breeding conditions, tissue/organ of origin, geographical origin, age, sex, test results for pathogenic agents, and physiological condition must be described
Culture medium constituents must be described in detail including information on exposure to materials of human or animal origin (serum, enzymes, hydrolysates)
Two-tiered cell bank system (MCB generating WCBs) is generally accepted as the most practical approach for continued product manufacture
MCB is generally made from an initial clone; WCBs are derived from one or more MCB containers; newly prepared WCBs must be appropriately qualified by characterisation and testing
Banking procedures must describe type of banking system, container types, closure systems, preparation methods, cryoprotectants, media, and cryopreservation/storage conditions
Procedures to avoid microbial contamination and cross-contamination by other cell types must be described including documentation and labelling systems for traceability
Catastrophic event protection measures must be described: redundant storage in multiple freezers, back-up power, automatic LN2 fill, storage at remote sites, and MCB regeneration procedures
Tests of identity must be performed on MCB to confirm banked cell is what it is represented to be; limited identity testing on each WCB
For metazoan cells, isoenzyme analysis is sufficient to confirm species of origin; alternatives include banding cytogenetics, species-specific antisera, DNA analysis (RFLP, VNTR, STR)
MCB and WCB must be assessed for biological purity (freedom from adventitious microbial agents and adventitious cellular contaminants)
Bioburden (bacteria and fungi) testing must be performed on individual containers (1% of total, not less than 2 containers) of MCB and WCB per Ph. Eur./JP/USP methods
Mycoplasma testing must be performed on MCB and WCB using both agar/broth media procedures and indicator cell culture procedures
Virus testing of cell substrates must detect wide spectrum of viruses using appropriate screening tests and specific tests based on cultivation history
Cell substrate stability must be evaluated at minimum two time points: cells with minimal subcultivations and cells at/beyond the limit of in vitro cell age for production
For rDNA cell lines, coding sequence consistency of expression construct must be verified in cells cultivated to the limit of in vitro cell age or beyond
Banked cell stability under storage conditions must be monitored: viability upon reconstitution verified when thawed for production, WCB preparation, or at defined intervals
For new or uncharacterised diploid cell substrates, diploid karyology confirmation and tumorigenic potential must be established using cells from the MCB
Karyology and tumorigenicity testing is not necessary for highly purified products that contain no cells, provided residual host cell DNA limits are consistently met
Primary cell substrates: tissue must be from healthy animals from closed SPF colonies; animals must be quarantined and not previously used for experimental studies
All reagents of human or animal origin used in primary cell substrate preparation must be identified and tested for absence of detectable contaminants and adventitious agents
For microbial cells, species, strain, genotypic and phenotypic characteristics, pathogenicity, toxin production, and biohazard information must be described
For E. coli where variety of strains may be used, biological characterisation methods such as phage typing should be considered as supplementary identity tests

comparability.description: Comparability of biotech/biological products subject to manufacturing process changes

rules.requirement: Comparability exercise must determine whether quality, safety, and efficacy of drug product are maintained after manufacturing process changes through collection of relevant technical data
Comparability does not mean identical; products must be highly similar with any quality attribute differences sufficiently predictive of no adverse impact on safety or efficacy
Comparability determination can be based on analytical testing, biological assays, and in some cases nonclinical and clinical data
Product must be evaluated at process step most appropriate to detect changes; may require evaluation at multiple manufacturing stages (intermediate, drug substance, drug product)
Battery of tests for comparability must be carefully selected and optimised to maximise potential for detecting relevant differences; may require multiple analytical procedures per quality attribute

Comparability characterisation must address: physicochemical properties (including higher order structure), biological activity, immunochemical properties, purity/impurities/contaminants, and quantity
Following process change, manufacturer must attempt to determine that higher order structure (secondary, tertiary, quaternary) is maintained
For products with multiple biological activities, a set of relevant functional assays evaluating all relevant activities (enzymatic, receptor-mediated) must be considered
For antibodies/antibody-based products, immunochemical properties must be confirmed as comparable in post-change product
If purity/impurity profile differences observed, assess potential impact on safety and efficacy; new impurities must be identified and characterised
Widening of acceptance criteria post-change is generally not considered appropriate unless justified
Results within acceptance criteria but outside historical manufacturing control trends may indicate product differences warranting additional study or analysis
Any change with potential to alter protein structure, purity, or impurity profiles must be evaluated for impact on stability; real-time/real-temperature studies should be initiated
Accelerated and stress stability studies should be used to establish degradation profiles and provide direct comparison of pre- and post-change product
Post-change process must be re-evaluated and/or re-validated as appropriate; in-process controls including critical control points must ensure quality is maintained

Demonstration of state of control for post-change process must include: bioburden/viral safety testing of post-change cell banks, adventitious agent clearance, removal of residual HCP/DNA, and maintenance of purity
For approved products, appropriate number of post-change commercial-scale batches must be analysed to demonstrate consistent process performance
Side-by-side description summarising pre- and post-change manufacturing process must be prepared, clearly highlighting modifications and changes in controls
For late-stage development changes with no additional clinical studies planned, comparability exercise should be as comprehensive and thorough as for approved product

Nonclinical/clinical studies are warranted when: quality data are insufficient, relationship between quality attributes and safety/efficacy is not established, or quality differences are observed with possible adverse impact that cannot be excluded
Factors for determining need for nonclinical/clinical studies include: product complexity, structure-activity relationship, therapeutic window, immunogenicity risk, PK/PD relationship, mode of action
Comparability nonclinical/clinical studies may include PK, PD, efficacy, safety, immunogenicity, and pharmacovigilance studies; should be direct comparative studies where appropriate
Historical data must be monitored for potential drift of quality attributes following single or serial manufacturing process changes to confirm no unacceptable impact on safety/efficacy profiles
